item.title: The Hidden Cost of Abstractions
item.dc:creator: David Thomas
item.content:encoded:
Every abstraction is a bet. You are betting that the complexity you are hiding will never matter—that the leaky parts of your model will never be exposed to your users.

Sometimes you win that bet. A well-chosen abstraction pays dividends for years. Files as a uniform interface over many storage devices. Sockets as a uniform interface over many network protocols. The bet paid off.

But abstractions leak. Joel Spolsky wrote about this in 2002, and nothing has changed. TCP/IP leaks when you need to reason about latency. File system abstractions leak when you care about durability guarantees. HTTP abstractions leak under load.

The cost is not just the eventual leak. The cost is that abstractions hide information that might be necessary for good decisions. A developer who only knows the ORM layer cannot make good decisions about query performance. A developer who only knows the cloud SDK cannot make good decisions about cost.

Good engineering requires knowing when to abstract and when to reach through the abstraction. The most dangerous developers are those who believe the abstraction is the reality.

Choose abstractions deliberately. Know where they leak. Keep the option to remove them.
